tossups.question: This character tells the story of a tax collector who uses robes and a mask to train a lap dog to love the Keeper of the Seals. Lionel Trilling’s Sincerity and Authenticity builds on the use of this character in Phenomenology of Spirit, in which he illustrates the self-estranged “disintegrated soul.” This character froths at the mouth during his increasingly elaborate musical pantomimes, which start with a Locatelli sonata and climax with Jomelli’s Lamentations. In the last line, this character tells “Mister Philosopher” that “the man who’ll laugh last will laugh best” as he dashes off to the opera. This character mocks “wood-pushing” (*) chess players in the Café de la Regence in a text in which his lines are marked with “Lui,” or “Him.” For 10 points, what cynical relative of a Baroque composer titles a dialogue by Denis Diderot?
tossups.question_sanitized: This character tells the story of a tax collector who uses robes and a mask to train a lap dog to love the Keeper of the Seals. Lionel Trilling's Sincerity and Authenticity builds on the use of this character in Phenomenology of Spirit, in which he illustrates the self-estranged "disintegrated soul." This character froths at the mouth during his increasingly elaborate musical pantomimes, which start with a Locatelli sonata and climax with Jomelli's Lamentations. In the last line, this character tells "Mister Philosopher" that "the man who'll laugh last will laugh best" as he dashes off to the opera. This character mocks "wood-pushing" (*) chess players in the Cafe de la Regence in a text in which his lines are marked with "Lui," or "Him." For 10 points, what cynical relative of a Baroque composer titles a dialogue by Denis Diderot?
tossups.answer: Rameau’s Nephew [or Le Neveu de Rameau; prompt on Rameau; prompt on Lui until read; prompt on Him until read; prompt on the Nephew or Neveu]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Rameau's Nephew [or Le Neveu de Rameau; prompt on Rameau; prompt on Lui until read; prompt on Him until read; prompt on the Nephew or Neveu]

tossups.question: Two composers from this country described as “painters in sound” title a 2020 Vikingur Ólafsson piano album that includes a transcription of “The Arts and the Hours.” A female composer from this country wrote a set of harpsichord suites separated by barless, time signature-less “unmeasured preludes.” Baroque music from this country was often performed with “unequal notes,” which is similar to swung eighths. The opening movements of Bach’s orchestral suites are in a style of (*) overture from this country with a slow-fast, two-movement structure. “The Mysterious Barricades” is by a harpsichordist from this country who wrote The Art of Playing the Harpsichord. A composer at this country’s royal court contracted gangrene while conducting his Te Deum. For 10 points, name this country home to François Couperin and Jean-Philippe Rameau.
tossups.question_sanitized: Two composers from this country described as "painters in sound" title a 2020 Vikingur Olafsson piano album that includes a transcription of "The Arts and the Hours." A female composer from this country wrote a set of harpsichord suites separated by barless, time signature-less "unmeasured preludes." Baroque music from this country was often performed with "unequal notes," which is similar to swung eighths. The opening movements of Bach's orchestral suites are in a style of (*) overture from this country with a slow-fast, two-movement structure. "The Mysterious Barricades" is by a harpsichordist from this country who wrote The Art of Playing the Harpsichord. A composer at this country's royal court contracted gangrene while conducting his Te Deum. For 10 points, name this country home to Francois Couperin and Jean-Philippe Rameau.
tossups.answer: France (The first line refers to Debussy/Rameau. The second line refers to Élisabeth Jacquet de la Guerre.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: France (The first line refers to Debussy/Rameau. The second line refers to Elisabeth Jacquet de la Guerre.)

tossups.question: Krump dancers perform to music from one work by this composer in a film by Clément Cogitore, who is staging that work with the choreographer Bintou Dembélé at the Opéra national de Paris. Mercury and Eglé dance with the muse Terpsichore to celebrate their love in this composer’s Les fêtes d’Hébé. Mnemosyne and the title muse praise the recent victory at the Battle of Fontenoy during the Temple of Memory prologue to this composer’s Les fêtes de Polymnie. (+) Voltaire wrote the libretto to this composer’s piece Le temple de la Gloire, and a tragic opera by this composer contains the aria Tristes apprets. This composer based one of his opera-ballets on Racine’s (*) Phèdre, and the racist act Les Sauvages is from this composer’s Orientalist opera Les Indes Galantes. For 10 points, name this French Baroque composer of Castor et Pollux and Hippolyte et Aricie who rivaled Jean-Baptiste Lully during the Baroque era.
tossups.question_sanitized: Krump dancers perform to music from one work by this composer in a film by Clement Cogitore, who is staging that work with the choreographer Bintou Dembele at the Opera national de Paris. Mercury and Egle dance with the muse Terpsichore to celebrate their love in this composer's Les fetes d'Hebe. Mnemosyne and the title muse praise the recent victory at the Battle of Fontenoy during the Temple of Memory prologue to this composer's Les fetes de Polymnie. (+) Voltaire wrote the libretto to this composer's piece Le temple de la Gloire, and a tragic opera by this composer contains the aria Tristes apprets. This composer based one of his opera-ballets on Racine's (*) Phedre, and the racist act Les Sauvages is from this composer's Orientalist opera Les Indes Galantes. For 10 points, name this French Baroque composer of Castor et Pollux and Hippolyte et Aricie who rivaled Jean-Baptiste Lully during the Baroque era.

tossups.question: In an opera by this man, Zima and Adario pledge their love for each other before a peace pipe ceremony; that opera by this man also contains the act "The Generous Turk." He cut a prologue that celebrated the War of the Polish Succession from the revival of an opera in which Telaira sings "Tristes apprêts" (treest ah-PREH) and is loved by a pair of twins, only one of whom is initially immortal. This man's operas were disparaged by fans of Pergolesi's La serva padrona in the "War of the Buffoons." He wrote one of the first explanations of tonal music in his Treatise on Harmony. For 10 points, name this French Baroque composer whose operas Castor et Pollux and Les indes galantes (lay ZAND gah-LONT) reimagined forms previously established by Jean-Baptiste Lully.
tossups.question_sanitized: In an opera by this man, Zima and Adario pledge their love for each other before a peace pipe ceremony; that opera by this man also contains the act "The Generous Turk." He cut a prologue that celebrated the War of the Polish Succession from the revival of an opera in which Telaira sings "Tristes apprets" (treest ah-PREH) and is loved by a pair of twins, only one of whom is initially immortal. This man's operas were disparaged by fans of Pergolesi's La serva padrona in the "War of the Buffoons." He wrote one of the first explanations of tonal music in his Treatise on Harmony. For 10 points, name this French Baroque composer whose operas Castor et Pollux and Les indes galantes (lay ZAND gah-LONT) reimagined forms previously established by Jean-Baptiste Lully.

tossups.question: In his preface to one of his collections, this composer defended the twelfth-bar transition from C sharp to D flat in the second section of one of the pieces. The left hand constantly crosses over the right, creating the illusion of the title deformity, in this composer's piece "The Three Hands." This man composed several tambourins, the best-known of which is the penultimate movement of his suite in E minor. Under the opening notes "G, G, G, G, G, G, B flat, D," this man wrote the words "co co co co co co co dai," imitating the (*) clucking of a hen. Princess Phani is courted by the rivals Don Carlos and Huascar, while a volcano erupts, in the Incas of Peru section of this composer's ballet-heroique Les indes galantes. One of this composer's operas ends with Jupiter decreeing that the protagonists can share their immortality, and another is based on a libretto by the composer's contemporary Jean Racine. For 10 points, name this baroque French composer of Castor et Pollux and Hippolyte et Aricie.
tossups.question_sanitized: In his preface to one of his collections, this composer defended the twelfth-bar transition from C sharp to D flat in the second section of one of the pieces. For 10 points, name this baroque French composer of Castor et Pollux and Hippolyte et Aricie.

tossups.question: The title character of this work distinguishes between literal and figurative ass-kissing when explaining why he doesn’t want to kiss Adelaide Hus’s ass. It includes the story of the renegade of Avignon, who lived with a Jew. One character in this work claims that Racine would have been richer as a grocer or a silk merchant than as a playwright, to which his interlocutor replies that Racine’s plays will make people cry a thousand years hence. The title character of this work explains how he taught accompaniment despite not understanding it, and later he performs a spirited imitation of the musical works of Duni. The narrator of this work is called “Mister Philosopher” by the title character, and he enjoys watching chess players in the Café de la Regence. For 10 points, name this philosophical dialogue by Denis Diderot, named for a relative of the composer of Les Indes Galantes.
tossups.answer: Rameau’s Nephew, or the Second Satire [or Le Neveu de Rameau ou La Satire seconde]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Rameau's Nephew, or the Second Satire [or Le Neveu de Rameau ou La Satire seconde]

One character in this work claims that Racine would have been richer as a grocer or a silk merchant than as a playwright, to which his interlocutor replies that Racine's plays will make people cry a thousand years hence. The title character of this work explains how he taught accompaniment despite not understanding it, and later he performs a spirited imitation of the musical works of Duni. The narrator of this work is called "Mister Philosopher" by the title character, and he enjoys watching chess players in the Cafe de la Regence. For 10 points, name this philosophical dialogue by Denis Diderot, named for a relative of the composer of Les Indes Galantes.

tossups.question: The wealthy financier La Poupliniere patronized this man, who composed "La Timide" and "L'Indiscrete" among other Pieces de clavecin en concerts. Telaira sings the aria "Tristes apprets, pales flambeaux" in one of this man's operas, while another has four acts in Turkey, Peru, Persia, and North America. This man's music was held up against the "corrupting" influence of Italian opera during the "War of the Buffoons," though he had earlier been controversial for the techniques he promoted in his Treatise on Harmony. This composer's operas include Les Indes Galantes and Castor et Pollux. For 10 points, name this Baroque composer who, after the death of Lully, was the dominant operatic composer in France.
tossups.question_sanitized: The wealthy financier La Poupliniere patronized this man, who composed "La Timide" and "L'Indiscrete" among other Pieces de clavecin en concerts. Telaira sings the aria "Tristes apprets, pales flambeaux" in one of this man's operas, while another has four acts in Turkey, Peru, Persia, and North America. This man's music was held up against the "corrupting" influence of Italian opera during the "War of the Buffoons," though he had earlier been controversial for the techniques he promoted in his Treatise on Harmony. This composer's operas include Les Indes Galantes and Castor et Pollux. For 10 points, name this Baroque composer who, after the death of Lully, was the dominant operatic composer in France.

tossups.question: This work’s title character tells of his time “under the leaky barrel” and standing by the statue of Memnon. Other topics include whether Diogenes would have danced the pantomime and a hypochondriac robot blinded by a nightcap. Michel Foucault’s Madness and Civilization claims this work was incomprehensible to its contemporaries, such as Palissot. It opens with the narrator watching chess players at Regency Park, where he encounters a man who is pained to listen to Les Indes Galantes because it reminds him of his artistic inferiority. Structured as a dialogue between “Me” and “Him,” for 10 points, name this work by Denis Diderot about a famous composer’s relative.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Rameau's Nephew or Le Neveau de Rameau

Structured as a dialogue between "Me" and "Him," for 10 points, name this work by Denis Diderot about a famous composer's relative.

bonuses.leadin: This word precedes “continuo” to describe the portion of a Baroque music ensemble that supplies the harmonic accompaniment. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This word precedes "continuo" to describe the portion of a Baroque music ensemble that supplies the harmonic accompaniment. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this word that follows “figured” in the name of a notation system that uses numbers to indicate what intervals to stack from the bottom up.

bonuses.leadin: The allegorical prologue of an opera by this composer is set in Hebe's palace, where Bellona intrudes, leading Cupid to depart from Europe. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French composer of Les Indes galantes and such tragédie en musique as Hippolyte et Aricie and Castor et Pollux.

Like Rameau's Les fetes d'Hebe, Les Indes galantes is in a form combining opera with this other theatrical genre. At the Paris Opera in 1832, Marie Taglioni became the first of its practitioners to don a romantic tutu.

This man's tribute opera Les muses galantes failed to delight Rameau, who feuded with this Geneva-born creator of The Village Soothsayer during the Querelle des Bouffons and attacked his Encyclopedie articles on music.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about pieces that are quoted in Saint-Saëns’s (“san-sawns’s”)The Carnival of the Animals, for 10 points each.
parts: The main melody of the movement “Tortoises” is an extremely slowed-down version of the cancan from this French composer’s comic opera, Orpheus in the Underworld.

One of the two lightweight melodies quoted in the “Elephants” movement is the “Ballet des sylphes” (“ballet day SILF”) from this Berlioz “dramatic legend,” which also includes the “Rakoczy (“RAH-ko-tsee”) March.”

Fittingly, the movement “Hens and Roosters” quotes “La Poule” (“pool”) from one of this composer’s collections of keyboard suites, which also includes “Les Triolets” (“leh trio-LAY”) and “Les Sauvages” (“leh so-VAZH”).
answers: La damnation de Faust, Op. 24 [or The Damnation of Faust; reject “Faust”]
answers_sanitized: La damnation de Faust, Op. 24 [or The Damnation of Faust; reject "Faust"]

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer some questions about the literary appearances of the phrase, “He who laughs last, laughs best.”
parts: The phrase may have originated from an early Jacobean play titled for a Prince of this holiday. Jacob Marley’s ghost is one of the many spirits that appear in an 1843 novella about this holiday.

After he is asked to help his sister-in-law, Mr. Barnard calms himself down by saying the phrase in this author’s play The Country House. This author’s play The Relapse is a sequel to Colley Cibber’s play Love’s Last Shift.

This work ends with the title character, called “Him” throughout the work, saying the phrase. This philosophical dialogue is set in the Regency Café where the narrator likes to watch people play chess.
answers: Rameau’s Nephew, or the Second Satire [or The Nephew of Rameau; or Le Neveu de Rameau ou La Satire seconde] (by Denis Diderot)
answers_sanitized: Christmas [accept The Christmas Prince or A Christmas Carol]

bonuses.leadin: Marin Marais (“mah-RAN mah-RAY”) wrote over 500 compositions for this instrument. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this instrument for which Antoine and Jean-Baptiste Forqueray wrote a collection of suites that have been recorded by Paolo Pandolfo. Jordi Savall is another modern player of this fretted instrument.

This composer wrote a follow-up to his Pièces de clavecin (“p’YES duh clav-SAN”) that uses a viol in addition to the solo harpsichord. This composer succeeded Lully as the leading French opera composer.
Forqueray and Rameau were composers from this era of music, which introduced many innovations following the Renaissance period. This era’s other composers included Antonio Vivaldi and J. S. Bach.
answers: viol (“vile”) [or viola da gamba; or gamba; do not accept or prompt on “viola” or “viola da braccio”]

parts: Name these two opera composers. One of them developed tragédie en musique (“tra-zhay-DEE on myoo-ZEEK”) in operas he worked on with Philippe Quinault (“kee-NO”), while the other came to prominence with a Treatise on Harmony.
Lully and Rameau are the two master French opera composers of this musical era of the 17th and early 18th centuries, which preceded the classical era.

bonuses.leadin: Identify the following about the career of computer animation pioneer John Whitney, for 10 points each.
parts: Many of Whitney’s animations used this photographic technique, in which a movable slide with an incision is placed between the camera and the object to be photographed, producing distorted effects. Douglas Trumbull used this technique in the Star Gate sequence in 2001: A Space Odyssey.

bonuses.leadin: The second of this musical work’s three movements is itself subdivided into three further movements: “Through the Streets and Roads,” “The Perfumes of the Night,” and “The Morning of a Festival Day.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this work whose three-part second movement is titled “Iberia.” André Caplet may or may not have had a hand in its first movement, “Gigues.”

Debussy placed the instruction enchaînez at the end of the “Perfumes” section of “Iberia.” Enchaînez is the French equivalent of this Italian word that indicates two movements should not be separated by a pause.

Claude Debussy’s first book of Images, for solo piano, includes an “Homage to” this earlier French composer and draws its melody from this man’s opera Castor et Pollux, which includes the aria “Sejour de l’eternelle paix.”
answers: Images pour orchestre [or Images for orchestra; accept Images Book 3 or Set 3 or Third Set; prompt on Images by asking “for what instrumentation?”]
answers_sanitized: Images pour orchestre [or Images for orchestra; accept Images Book 3 or Set 3 or Third Set; prompt on Images by asking "for what instrumentation?"]

parts_sanitized: Name this work whose three-part second movement is titled "Iberia." Andre Caplet may or may not have had a hand in its first movement, "Gigues."

Debussy placed the instruction enchainez at the end of the "Perfumes" section of "Iberia." Enchainez is the French equivalent of this Italian word that indicates two movements should not be separated by a pause.

Claude Debussy's first book of Images, for solo piano, includes an "Homage to" this earlier French composer and draws its melody from this man's opera Castor et Pollux, which includes the aria "Sejour de l'eternelle paix."

bonuses.leadin: Pieces for this instrument include “The Mysterious Barricades” and “The Tic-Toc-Choc.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this keyboard instrument, a predecessor of the piano, whose individual keys pluck a string upon being depressed.
This French Baroque composer wrote a treatise on The Art of Playing the Harpsichord. He was known as “le Grand” to differentiate him from family members such as his uncle Louis.

This other French composer wrote the opera-ballet Les Indes Galantes (luh ZEEND gah-LAHNT), a 1722 Treatise on Harmony, and three books of Pièces de clavecin (“piece” duh CLAH-vuh-SENG), the last of which includes an A minor gavotte with six variations.

parts_sanitized: Name this keyboard instrument, a predecessor of the piano, whose individual keys pluck a string upon being depressed.

This other French composer wrote the opera-ballet Les Indes Galantes (luh ZEEND gah-LAHNT), a 1722 Treatise on Harmony, and three books of Pieces de clavecin ("piece" duh CLAH-vuh-SENG), the last of which includes an A minor gavotte with six variations.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about musical treatises written during the Baroque era, for 10 points each:
parts: This composer used arithmetic division to show that thirds and fifths could be used to ‘generate’ all other intervals in his Treatise On Harmony. He also found success with his operas Castor et Pollux and Hippolyte et Aricie.

Rameau’s method of generating intervals is most similar to this tuning system, which is based on the ratios of small whole numbers. In the 19th century, Hermann von Helmholtz launched attacks on equal temperament in favor of this system.
This earlier German composer and theorist wrote the three-volume treatise Syntagma Musicum, considered one of the main sources of seventeenth-century theory.

This earlier German composer and theorist wrote the three-volume treatise Syntagma Musicum, considered one of the main sources of seventeenth-century theory.

bonuses.leadin: In his Treatise on Harmony, this composer introduced the notion of the “fundamental bass”. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this French composer from the time of Louis XV, who wrote the operas Hippolyte et Aricie and Castor et Pollux.
Rameau’s “lyric tragedies” were supported by one side of this aesthetic conflict between French and Italian opera fans of the 1750’s. It was started by a performance of Pergolesi’s La serva padrona.

Rameau was also a prolific composer for this keyboard instrument, which works by plucking a string when a key is depressed. François Couperin wrote for this instrument, which was played by Wanda Landowska.
answers: Querelle des Bouffons [or War of the Buffoons; or Guerre des Bouffons; or War/Quarrel of the Comic Actors; or Guerre des Coins; or War/Quarrel of the Corners]
answers_sanitized: Querelle des Bouffons [or War of the Buffoons; or Guerre des Bouffons; or War/Quarrel of the Comic Actors; or Guerre des Coins; or War/Quarrel of the Corners]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In his Treatise on Harmony, this composer introduced the notion of the "fundamental bass". For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this French composer from the time of Louis XV, who wrote the operas Hippolyte et Aricie and Castor et Pollux.
Rameau's "lyric tragedies" were supported by one side of this aesthetic conflict between French and Italian opera fans of the 1750's. It was started by a performance of Pergolesi's La serva padrona.

Rameau was also a prolific composer for this keyboard instrument, which works by plucking a string when a key is depressed. Francois Couperin wrote for this instrument, which was played by Wanda Landowska.

bonuses.leadin: This man is often credited with codifying the theory of tonal music in his Treatise on Harmony. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this French composer who, though perhaps better known for his operas, also composed much harpsichord music like “La Rappel des Oiseaux” and “La Poule,” in addition to Pieces de clavecin en concerts.

Rameau is sometimes considered the successor to this Italian-born French composer credited with bringing opera to France. You probably know him better for dying of gangrene after he stabbed his foot with a conducting staff.

Both Lully and Rameau are famous for their takes on this dance in simple meter, which like the menuet and the bouree was often placed in the fifth section of the baroque suite, between the sarabande and gigue. Bach’s 5th and 6th cello suites both feature these dances.

Both Lully and Rameau are famous for their takes on this dance in simple meter, which like the menuet and the bouree was often placed in the fifth section of the baroque suite, between the sarabande and gigue. Bach's 5th and 6th cello suites both feature these dances.

bonuses.leadin: The title character of this novel discusses the Calas family and declares, “He that laughs last laughs best.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel whose narrator ventures into the Regency Café and strikes up a conversation with the relative of a composer.
This Frenchman wrote Rameau’s Nephew. A valet declares that everything that happens is written on a “great scroll” in this man’s novel Jacques the Fatalist.
Diderot is better known for co-editing this reference book with Jean Le Rond d’Alembert.
answers_sanitized: Rameau's Nephew [or Le Neveu de Rameau]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The title character of this novel discusses the Calas family and declares, "He that laughs last laughs best." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this novel whose narrator ventures into the Regency Cafe and strikes up a conversation with the relative of a composer.
This Frenchman wrote Rameau's Nephew. A valet declares that everything that happens is written on a "great scroll" in this man's novel Jacques the Fatalist.
Diderot is better known for co-editing this reference book with Jean Le Rond d'Alembert.

bonuses.leadin: His keyboard works include Les fastes de la grande et ancienne Mxnxstrxndxsx, which depicts the collapse of a troupe of traveling musicians due to the confusion caused by drunkards, monkeys, and bears. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this composer who collected his keyboard works, including “Le tic-toc-choc” and “Les baricades mistérieuses,” in twenty-seven ordres.

Another French Baroque writer of keyboard music was this composer of La Dauphine and several collections of Pièces de clavecin. He also wrote an influential Treatise on Harmony and composed Hippolyte et Aricie.
This other composer’s pièces de clavecin include “Les trois cadences,” named for the triple trill that was the first of its kind in French music, and the repetitive “Le coucou.”

This other composer's pieces de clavecin include "Les trois cadences," named for the triple trill that was the first of its kind in French music, and the repetitive "Le coucou."
